Cartwheel (Roue) — quantitative analysis of three technical criteria

AlexiaCamillaMattiasNoemi · 5 takes per subject  ·  Motive @ 360 Hz  ·  Skeleton-only (no rigid bodies)  ·  Criteria adapted from the student poster (Messerli, Scanio, Olivieri, Rytz)
Across the four subjects (5 takes each, 20 cartwheels total), the three technical criteria from the poster do not split the cohort into two clean pairs of two. Each subject shows a distinct technical signature, and no single criterion classifies them in the same order: Alexia is the cleanest overall (low body lean, widest leg split, lowest path RMS), Mattias covers the most ground but with the narrowest leg split of the four, Camilla sits in the middle on every metric, Noemi shows the most off-axis cartwheel (highest body lean, shortest travel). The poster's intuitive 2-expert / 2-novice framing partly holds (Alexia clearly stronger than Noemi) but Camilla and Mattias swap places depending on which criterion you weight.

1. Trajectory linearity (criterion 1)

2. Cartwheel motion form (comic-strip view)

3. Body-axis stability (criterion 2)

4. Leg differentiation (criterion 3)

5. Within-subject consistency

6. Summary metrics

PlayerC1 — RMS lateral (cm)C1′ — RMS / travel (%)C2 — body lean (°)C3 — leg split @ inv (°)Travel distance (m)Duration (s)
Alexia0.7 ± 0.30.4 ± 0.13.2° ± 1.3125° ± 21.91 ± 0.051.06 ± 0.06
Camilla1.7 ± 0.31.1 ± 0.225.0° ± 2.2109° ± 71.57 ± 0.111.14 ± 0.13
Mattias1.7 ± 0.20.8 ± 0.125.8° ± 4.464° ± 51.99 ± 0.051.18 ± 0.11
Noemi6.3 ± 0.34.6 ± 0.442.9° ± 1.2106° ± 141.36 ± 0.071.30 ± 0.09
Five takes per subject, median ± std (median is more robust at n=5; per the guide §11). Cells are tinted: green = better on this criterion, red = worse. Duration is left uncoloured (faster ≠ technically better — slow controlled cartwheels can be cleaner).

7. Key observations

  • C1 — RMS lateral (cm): Alexia lowest (0.7 ± 0.3) — Noemi highest (6.3 ± 0.3)
  • C1′ — RMS / travel (%): Alexia lowest (0.4 ± 0.1) — Noemi highest (4.6 ± 0.4)
  • C2 — body lean (°): Alexia lowest (3.2° ± 1.3) — Noemi highest (42.9° ± 1.2)
  • C3 — leg split @ inv (°): Alexia highest (125° ± 2) — Mattias lowest (64° ± 5)
  • Travel distance (m): Mattias highest (1.99 ± 0.05) — Noemi lowest (1.36 ± 0.07)
Synthesis. The headline finding is that the four subjects split into four distinct technical signatures, not two pairs of two. (i) Alexia is the cleanest cartwheel of the cohort on every criterion — path RMS 0.7 cm vs the others' 1.7–6.3 cm, body lean 3° vs 25–43°, leg split 125° vs 64–109°. The gap between Alexia and the other three is consistent and large. (ii) Mattias's signature is specific and counter-intuitive: he covers the most ground (travel 1.99 m, longest of the four) but does it with his legs essentially together (split 64°, narrowest of the four — half Alexia's). The poster would call this 'long but the legs don't form arcs', and figure 4 makes the point visually. He is fast and linear (mid-pack on path RMS) but his body lean (26°) is in the same range as Camilla's. He looks like a strong cartwheeler from C1 + travel and a weaker one from C3. (iii) Camilla and Noemi differ less on technique than on commitment to the gesture: Noemi's higher body lean (43°) and lateral path drift (6.3 cm) look like the cartwheel of someone who's protecting against falling sideways. Camilla's metrics sit between Alexia's and Noemi's on every criterion. The poster's binary expert / novice framing captures the Alexia ↔ Noemi extremes well but doesn't give Mattias and Camilla a fair description — they are in fact dissimilar to each other along different axes. (iv) The absolute and relative C1 metrics tell slightly different stories in the middle of the cohort: as percentage of travel, Alexia is at 0.4%, Mattias 0.9%, Camilla 1.1%, Noemi 4.6%. The relative metric edges Mattias above Camilla (his cartwheel covers more ground for the same lateral drift) where the absolute ties them at 1.7 cm. Both columns are reported in the summary table. Alexia and Noemi remain the clear extremes either way.
Limits of this analysis. Limits of this analysis. n = 4 subjects, 5 takes each. The technical signatures describe these four individuals, not any broader category of expert vs novice gymnasts. The student poster's intuitive 2 + 2 framing is partially supported (Alexia vs Noemi as the clear extremes) but Camilla and Mattias swap places depending on which criterion you weight, so the quantitative analysis suggests four distinct signatures rather than two pairs. The poster did not name which subjects were labelled expert vs novice, so the Alexia-and-? pairing was tested empirically rather than imposed.

Data-quality probe: worst NaN coverage on a key bone (Hip / Head / hands / feet / toes) inside the ROI was 0.72% (longest consecutive gap 1 frames). Well below the 1% threshold; no take corrupted by marker dropouts. Outlier check (±40% from subject median, n=5): flagged Alexia/Alexia_3 on rms_lateral (0.02, +168% from median 0.01); Camilla/Camilla_5 on rms_lateral (0.01, +46% from median 0.02); Mattias/Mattias_1 on rms_lateral (0.03, +108% from median 0.02); Mattias/Mattias_2 on rms_lateral (0.01, +51% from median 0.02). The outlier rule applies the noise-floor escape from guide §7 step 5: a deviation that exceeds 40% of the subject's median is only flagged if its absolute size also exceeds the sensor noise floor (~5 mm for positional RMS, ~3° for orientations). Without this escape, Alexia's body-lean and path-RMS would have produced spurious flags purely because her medians are small (sub-cm path RMS, sub-5° lean). All 20 takes also passed the per-take validity check (all five canonical phases detected, head dropped below 0.7 m at inversion, duration in [0.5, 3.0] s).
Pipeline: mocap.py + report.py from the project knowledge base. Analysis per guide §6.4 (whole-body travelling rotation): POI = lead-hand touchdown, ROI = [lead-hand TD, trail-foot TD]. Vertical Y, quaternions (x, y, z, w). Smoothing: Savitzky-Golay window 21, polynomial 3.
